findings.location: N/A - no mention of TurboTax Free/Free Edition product on page
findings.reasoning: This page is a Spanish-language landing page for TurboTax Expert Full Service (bilingual tax expert service). Reviewing the full text, there is no mention of 'TurboTax Free', 'Free Edition', '$0', or any free filing product offer. The only uses of 'gratis'/'free' relate to 'Comenzar es 100% gratis. Paga solo cuando realicemos la presentación' which refers to starting the Full Service process being free (you only pay when filing is completed) - this is a different concept (free to start, not a free tax product/edition) and does not reference the TurboTax Free Edition product that qualifies under the ~37% simple-1040 eligibility disclosure. The footer links include 'Free Edition tax filing' and 'Free military tax filing discount' as navigation links, but these are just site navigation menu items, not marketing mentions of the Free product on this page's content that would need the eligibility disclosure. Since no substantive mention of TurboTax Free (the product) occurs in the page's marketing content, the rule's trigger condition is not met. Counter-reading considered: one could argue the footer nav link 'Free Edition tax filing' constitutes a 'mention' of TurboTax Free, triggering the rule. However, this is a bare navigational hyperlink label within a large sitemap-style footer, not a marketing claim or offer being promoted on this page; treating every footer nav link as a triggering 'mention' would be overly broad and inconsistent with the rule's intent to disclose eligibility limits when Free is actively promoted as an offer. Given the ambiguity, I lean toward not_applicable but flag this could be a judgment call.

The rule triggers whenever TurboTax Free is mentioned. The phrase "Start for Free" in the top navigation is a Free offer reference tied to TurboTax tax-filing products, so the trigger is met. Directly underneath this navigation item there is no eligibility disclosure text of any kind, let alone the required disclosure about ~37% of filers and simple 1040 eligibility. Therefore axis A is not compliant. Because there is no disclosure language present, it also does not match the approved library entry on axis B. A possible counter-argument is that generic marketing CTAs like navigation labels may be exempt or that the presence of other product links in the footer changes the requirement, but the rule as written does not carve out navigation or CTAs and applies whenever TurboTax Free is mentioned, so this is treated as a violation.

The footer list under "Online Software Products" includes "Free Edition tax filing", which is a clear reference to the TurboTax Free Edition product. That satisfies the trigger for R-01. Immediately underneath this list item there is no disclosure regarding percentage of filers who qualify or the simple Form 1040 limitation. No such disclosure appears elsewhere directly under this specific Free Edition mention. Thus the rule’s requirement for a specific eligibility disclosure right underneath is not met (axis A false). Since there is no eligibility wording at all, it also cannot match the approved library entry (axis B false). A potential counter-reading is that the linked Free Edition product page might contain the required disclosure, but the rule applies to the page where the mention occurs, not the destination page, so that does not cure the omission here.

Trigger: The footer explicitly mentions the TurboTax Free Edition (“Free Edition tax filing”), which triggers R-01. Requirement: The required eligibility disclosure (“~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only…”) does not appear right underneath this mention (or anywhere nearby on the page). Therefore Axis A is not met. Because no disclosure text is present, Axis B cannot match the approved library entry. Strongest counter-reading: One might argue this is merely a navigational link and the disclosure can live on the destination page, or that the Spanish body text’s “Comenzar es 100 % gratis” is a generic ‘start free’ claim for Full Service, not “TurboTax Free.” However, R-01 is triggered by any on-page mention of TurboTax Free/Free Edition and requires the disclosure directly underneath the mention on the same page, which is absent here.
